tag: 25 min · strip the goal down to the motivator underneath
title: Find the Real Fuel
intro: Most stated goals are socially acceptable covers for something rawer underneath. The cover-goal rarely sustains effort past the first hard month. This exercise strips the cover off and finds what's actually pulling.

quote.text: The strongest fuel is rarely the thing you tell other people you're after. It's the thing you don't quite want to say out loud.

deliverable: A one-sentence statement of the real motivator underneath your current stated goal. Written plainly. Kept private.

exercises.title: State the Goal Plainly
exercises.sub: 5 min · the version you'd put on a performance review
prompt: Write your current primary goal — the thing you've been telling yourself (and maybe others) you're working toward. One sentence. Use the language you'd use in a review, a resume, or a dinner-party answer to 'what are you working on?'

guidance.title: Use the sanitized version first
guidance.body: This is the wrapper. It doesn't have to be honest yet. 'Become a VP.' 'Launch the product.' 'Get to a million in revenue.' The generic version is exactly what you want here — that's the surface layer we're going to strip.

exercises.title: The Five Whys
exercises.sub: 15 min · drill down until you hit something that makes you slightly uncomfortable
prompt: Take the sanitized goal from 1.1. Ask 'why do I want that?' and write a one-sentence answer. Then ask 'why do I want that?' about your answer. Five times.
Each answer should go one layer deeper — not sideways. If the layer below sounds almost identical to the layer above, you're dodging. Push again.
The real answer usually arrives somewhere between layer 3 and layer 5, and it usually arrives with a small physical flinch.

guidance.title: Signals you've hit the real one
guidance.body: Slight discomfort writing it. Sounds petty, selfish, or raw when you read it back. Would sound bad in a job interview. Would be accurate in a private journal. These are all good signs. The respectable motivators rarely sustain work; the raw ones do.

guidance.title: Common layer-3 traps
guidance.body: 'To help people' / 'because it's meaningful' / 'to make a difference' — these are usually cover goals too. Keep asking why. The person those things serve is usually you, in disguise.

guidance.title: Let it be small and specific
guidance.body: The real fuel is often embarrassingly small. 'So my cousin will finally take me seriously.' 'So I never have to explain myself at a family dinner again.' 'So the person who said I wasn't capable has to update their model of me.' Smallness doesn't mean weakness — the small specific motivators are the sharpest.

exercises.watch_out: Staying philosophical. If your layer-5 answer sounds like a TED talk, it isn't the real layer. Go again. The real answer is something you'd hesitate to say aloud at a work lunch.

exercises.title: Write the Real Sentence
prompt: Write the real motivator as one sentence. Start with 'The actual thing I want is...' — no softening, no qualifying, no making it sound more respectable. Plain language.
Then ask: would reading this sentence on a flat day move you? If yes, keep it. If not, the five whys weren't deep enough — go back and push further.

guidance.title: Keep this one private
guidance.body: This sentence isn't for a talk, a post, or a colleague. It's for you. The value comes from its honesty, and honesty doesn't survive an audience. Store it somewhere only you will see.

exercises.tip: Re-run this exercise every six months. The real motivator shifts as you change, and the version that was potent last year may have gone dull.
